behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: real OS filesystem watcher behavior (recursive watch + emitted path). The failure can occur at the watcher/OS boundary (e.g., daemon only receives root-level events). MOCK-HIDE applies because a mocked watcher can emit subdirectory events even if real recursive watch is misconfigured. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is only about filesystem watching/processing, with no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temporary input/output directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced for that nested file path without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: local dedup decision logic (same path + time window). The behavior text focuses on policy correctness, not OS events or watcher semantics. A deterministic clock/input setup can expose real failures in keying/window math. Rejected higher levels with behavior evidence: no requirement here about real filesystem delivery (contrast with behaviors explicitly mentioning OS events), no AI/ML dependency, no multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedup component directly with a controlled clock: submit one path, resubmit same path inside the configured window (expect discard), then outside the window (expect accept), and verify different paths are not deduped.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: real OS event emission patterns for one write. The behavior explicitly says "operating system emits multiple filesystem events," so REAL-INFRA is required. MOCK-HIDE applies because synthetic event mocks may not reproduce real burst/coalescing/ordering edge cases. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML quality judgment and no chained agentic steps in the behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon with a real watched directory, perform file-write patterns known to trigger multiple watcher events (e.g., write+rename/flush), capture processing invocations, and assert only one successful processing/output for that logical file write.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: OS filesystem lifecycle events (delete/recreate directory) and watcher reattachment behavior. This failure occurs at real watcher/OS boundaries and often depends on platform-specific watcher invalidation. MOCK-HIDE applies because mocked watchers can unrealistically preserve subscriptions across delete/recreate. Rejected higher levels: behavior text concerns directory watch continuity only, with no AI/ML output dependence and no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, delete the watched directory while running, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed successfully without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: queue ordering based on real file timestamp metadata under concurrent arrivals. While ordering logic is local, the real failure boundary includes filesystem timestamp resolution/semantics and event arrival races; in-process fakes can mask these. Rejected higher levels: behavior is about file ordering, not AI/ML judgment and not multi-agent sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on real filesystem, create several files near-simultaneously, record each file’s creation timestamp from filesystem metadata, and assert processing order matches the expected timestamp order (with deterministic tie-break rule if timestamps collide).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: real concurrent writer/watcher interaction and OS file visibility timing. The failure appears when watcher notifications race with ongoing writes; mocked dependencies frequently miss partial-write timing behavior. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is filesystem readiness gating only, with no AI/ML correctness dependency and no multi-agent workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real watched directory, start a slow/streaming write to a file (multiple chunks with delays), verify processing does not start during partial writes, then verify processing starts only after write completion and output reflects full final content.
